read-history-with-log-and-show.label: Read History with Log and Show
read-history-with-log-and-show.summary: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.

sketch0.title: Use the Log as a Table of Contents

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Scan the One-Line History
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Read the three-entry history of `community-site/` in compact form.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Read the timeline from newest checkpoint to oldest.

help.concept: git log --oneline compresses each snapshot to an identifier and subject so the project story is easy to scan.
help.hint_1: Focus on commit order and subject lines rather than machine-specific hashes.
help.hint_2: The newest entry should describe the result from this milestone.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.starterCode: Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Read the three-entry history of `community-site/` in compact form.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf community-site mkdir -p community-site git -C community-site init -q -b main git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local" cd community-site printf %s '

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Start community site' cd community-site printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"]; ' > nav.js cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add events navigation' cd community-site printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon. ' > README.md cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Clarify volunteer hours'

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Start community site' cd community-site printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"]; ' > nav.js cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add events navigation' cd community-site printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon. ' > README.md cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Clarify volunteer hours'

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Inspect the Latest Snapshot
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Use `git show --stat HEAD` to see the latest commit message and the files it changed.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Open the checkpoint named in the task and inspect its evidence.

help.concept: git show connects a commit’s message to the exact snapshot and patch it introduced.
help.hint_1: Choose a summary view for file shape or a full patch for line-level detail.
help.hint_2: Confirm that the snapshot contents support its message.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Inspect the Previous Snapshot
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The newest commit changed the README. Inspect `HEAD~1` to review the snapshot immediately before it.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Open the checkpoint named in the task and inspect its evidence.

help.concept: HEAD~1 names the first parent of the current commit, which is the snapshot immediately before HEAD in this linear history.
help.hint_1: Choose a summary view for file shape or a full patch for line-level detail.
help.hint_2: Confirm that the snapshot contents support its message.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Connect History to Current State
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Read the one-line history, inspect the latest snapshot, and finish with short status to confirm that inspection changed nothing.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Open the checkpoint named in the task and inspect its evidence.

help.concept: History inspection is read-only: log and show explain snapshots without changing the working tree.
help.hint_1: Choose a summary view for file shape or a full patch for line-level detail.
help.hint_2: Confirm that the snapshot contents support its message.

single-choice-1.title: HEAD in Git
single-choice-1.prompt: What does `HEAD` refer to in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Scan the repository timeline, open one checkpoint, understand `HEAD`, and move between overview and detail.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The first commit in the repository
options.c: The latest commit in the current branch

sketch0.title: Use the Log as a Table of Contents
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
The one-line log presents history as a compact timeline. Each row pairs a shortened identifier with a subject, and the newest checkpoint appears first.

The identifier helps Git select a commit; the message and order help a human understand the project. Do not memorize hashes—they differ between repositories and change when history changes.

Start with the log when you know a change happened but need to locate the checkpoint that recorded it.

Open the compact timeline with:

```bash
git log --oneline
```

Each row combines a shortened identifier with a subject. Read the subjects and order first; the identifiers are selectors, not facts to memorize.

sketch1.title: Open One Checkpoint
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
`git show` turns a selected commit from a line in the timeline into evidence. It can display author information, the subject, changed files, and the line-by-line patch.

A statistics view is useful for a quick shape: which files changed and how large was the edit? The full patch is useful when you need to judge the actual content.

Choose the amount of detail that answers the current question instead of reading every commit in full.

To inspect the newest checkpoint without opening its full patch, request its statistics:

```bash
git show --stat HEAD
```

`HEAD` selects the current commit. `--stat` summarizes the files and line counts while still showing the commit subject.

sketch2.title: Understand Where HEAD Points
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
`HEAD` is Git’s name for the commit currently checked out. On `main`, it normally points to the newest commit on that branch.

Think of it as “the snapshot I am standing on.” A new commit moves the branch and HEAD forward. Status compares the working tree and staging area with this current checkpoint.

That connection explains why history inspection and state inspection belong together.

Move one checkpoint backward with the relative name `HEAD~1`:

```bash
git show --stat HEAD~1
```

This does not change the checked-out files. It only asks Git to display the parent of the current commit.

sketch3.title: Move Between Overview and Detail
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
History reading works like a book: the log is the table of contents, and show opens one chapter. Begin with the overview, choose the checkpoint that matters, then inspect its details.

This is faster and safer than opening today’s files and guessing how they arrived there. It also lets you check whether a commit message accurately describes the snapshot.

A good history remains understandable at both levels.

A complete inspection moves from overview to detail and back to state:

```bash
git log --oneline
git show --stat HEAD
git status --short
```

The first two commands read history. The final status check proves that inspection itself changed nothing.
